quote: Deep RL agents could only solve the task when designed to explicitly predict action prediction errors that fire in the medial prefrontal cortex. When trained this way, they displayed signatures of metacontrol that closely resembled those observed in humans.

When deep RL agents were trained to over- or underestimate controllability, they developed behavioral pathologies partially matching those of humans who reported depressive, anxious, or compulsive traits

The theory holds that consciousness does not facilitate the emergence of evolutionary functions embedded in the biological firmware of the human mind because it saturates the system with internal language, symbolic analysis and continuous monitoring.
When conscious control decreases ancient systems emerge with clarity and precision.
Passivity in response to shock is not learned. It is the default, unlearned response to prolonged aversive events and it is mediated by the serotonergic activity of the dorsal raphe nucleus

It is the presence of control, not the absence of control, that is detected by prelimbic medial prefrontal circuits, leading to consequent prelimbic-mediated inhibition of stress-responsive brainstem structures such as the dorsal raphe nucleus

When deep RL agents were trained to over- or underestimate controllability, they developed behavioral pathologies partially matching those of humans who reported depressive, anxious, or compulsive traits on transdiagnostic questionnaires

After the prelimbic-dorsal raphe nucleus ACT circuit is activated a set of changes that require several hours occurs in this pathway and involves the formation of new proteins related to plasticity. This is now a circuit that EXPECTS control.
Overcontrol was associated with reduced neural response to errors versus correct responses in the bilateral dACC and insula, even when controlling for anxiety symptoms
Inducing a heightened state of subjective control buffers against the impact of later stress
Striatal D1 and D2 opponency allows animals to perform cost-benefit calculations across multiple scales

Overcontrol is a transdiagnostic phenotype characterized by too much self-control, behavioral and cognitive inflexibility, high inhibition of emotion, high detail-focused processing, and lack of social connectedness
The brain uses a limited, low-dimensional, set of control states. Limiting the number of control states comes at a cost: imperfect, imprecise, control
